ethical_notes: The Weimar Republic inherited the territorial losses of WWI imposed by the Treaty of Versailles (1919). Significant German-speaking populations were placed under foreign sovereignty, creating ethnic tensions exploited by later regimes.

name_variants.name: Weimar Republic
name_variants.context: Common English name derived from the city where the constitution was drafted

name_variants.name: Deutsches Reich
name_variants.context: Official name retained from the Kaiserreich

territory_changes.description: Alsace-Lorraine ceded to France under the Treaty of Versailles
Province of Posen ceded to newly reconstituted Poland
Northern Schleswig transferred to Denmark after plebiscite

sources.citation: Kolb, E. (2005). The Weimar Republic. Routledge.
Treaty of Versailles (1919), Part III: Political Clauses for Europe
Mommsen, H. (1996). The Rise and Fall of Weimar Democracy. University of North Carolina Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Nazi Germany perpetrated the Holocaust (Shoah), the systematic genocide of six million Jews, alongside the murder of Roma, Sinti, disabled persons, political opponents, homosexuals, and others — totaling an estimated 11-17 million civilians. This entity must always be presented with full documentation of these crimes. The term 'Third Reich' (Drittes Reich) is the regime's own propagandistic self-designation. Data includes the maximum territorial extent (1942) including annexed territories.

name_variants.name: Drittes Reich
name_variants.context: Propagandistic self-designation used by the Nazi regime
name_variants.source: Evans, R. (2003). The Coming of the Third Reich

territory_changes.description: Anschluss: forced annexation of Austria into the German Reich
Sudetenland annexed from Czechoslovakia after Munich Agreement
Military occupation and annexation of remaining Czech lands
Invasion and annexation of western Poland; beginning of WWII in Europe

Systematic extermination of European Jews (Shoah): approximately 6 million murdered in concentration and extermination camps. Additional millions of Roma, Sinti, disabled, political prisoners, and others killed.
Unconditional surrender of Nazi Germany; all annexed and occupied territories liberated by Allied forces

sources.citation: Evans, R.J. (2003-2008). The Third Reich Trilogy. Penguin.
Hilberg, R. (2003). The Destruction of the European Jews. Yale University Press.
Nuremberg Trial Proceedings, International Military Tribunal (1945-1946)

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The USSR perpetrated multiple mass atrocities including the Holodomor (engineered famine in Ukraine, 1932-33), the Great Purge, mass deportations of entire ethnic groups (Chechens, Crimean Tatars, Volga Germans, and others), and the Gulag system. These events must be documented with affected populations. The Soviet system also suppressed national identities and languages of constituent republics.

territory_changes.description: Formation of USSR from Russian SFSR, Ukrainian SSR, Byelorussian SSR, and Transcaucasian SFSR
Holodomor: engineered famine in Soviet Ukraine causing mass death through deliberate grain confiscation policies
Soviet invasion of Eastern Poland under Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact; subsequent annexation of Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania
Mass deportation of Chechens, Ingush, Crimean Tatars, Kalmyks, and other peoples to Central Asia. Hundreds of thousands died during transportation and exile.
Dissolution of the USSR; 15 independent successor states

sources.citation: Applebaum, A. (2003). Gulag: A History. Doubleday.
Conquest, R. (1986). The Harvest of Sorrow: Soviet Collectivization and the Terror-Famine. Oxford University Press.
Belavezha Accords (1991), Agreement establishing the Commonwealth of Independent States

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Yugoslavia's dissolution (1991-2001) involved multiple wars, ethnic cleansings, and the Srebrenica genocide (1995). The Ustasha regime in WWII-era Independent State of Croatia committed genocide against Serbs, Jews, and Roma. These events are documented in territory_changes. All ethnic perspectives are represented.

name_variants.name: Yugoslavia
name_variants.context: English name used throughout the state's existence

name_variants.name: Краљевина Срба, Хрвата и Словенаца
name_variants.context: Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes — original official name

name_variants.name: Социјалистичка Федеративна Република Југославија
name_variants.context: Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia — SFRY, post-WWII official name

territory_changes.description: Formation of the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes from former Austro-Hungarian, Serbian, and Montenegrin territories
Ustasha regime in the puppet Independent State of Croatia committed genocide against Serbs, Jews, and Roma in camps including Jasenovac
Breakup of Yugoslavia through declarations of independence triggering the Yugoslav Wars
Srebrenica genocide: Bosnian Serb forces killed over 8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys; classified as genocide by the ICTY and ICJ

sources.citation: Glenny, M. (1999). The Balkans: Nationalism, War and the Great Powers, 1804-1999. Viking.
ICTY, Prosecutor v. Radislav Krstić, Case IT-98-33 (2001) — Srebrenica genocide judgment
Lampe, J. (2000). Yugoslavia as History: Twice There Was a Country. Cambridge University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Czechoslovakia experienced the expulsion of approximately 3 million Sudeten Germans (Beneš Decrees, 1945-46), a massive ethnic cleansing. The state also endured the Soviet-led invasion of 1968 that crushed the Prague Spring democratic reform movement.

name_variants.name: Česko-Slovensko
name_variants.context: Hyphenated variant used in early period

territory_changes.description: Formation of Czechoslovakia from former Austro-Hungarian lands after WWI
Sudetenland ceded to Nazi Germany under the Munich Agreement, without Czechoslovak consent
Expulsion of approximately 3 million Sudeten Germans under the Beneš Decrees
Velvet Divorce: peaceful dissolution into Czech Republic and Slovakia

sources.citation: Heimann, M. (2009). Czechoslovakia: The State That Failed. Yale University Press.
Munich Agreement (1938), signed by Germany, Italy, France, United Kingdom
Beneš Decrees (1945), Presidential Decrees No. 108/1945 and No. 33/1945

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Portuguese Empire was one of the longest-lasting colonial empires. It was the first major European colonial power to engage in the transatlantic slave trade. Colonial exploitation in Africa (Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau, Cape Verde, São Tomé) persisted until 1975. The boundary_geojson represents metropolitan Portugal only; colonial territories spanned four continents. Macau was returned to China in 1999.

territory_changes.description: Capture of Ceuta marks the beginning of Portuguese overseas expansion
Arrival of Pedro Álvares Cabral and beginning of Portuguese colonization of Brazil

territory_changes.region: Angola, Mozambique, Guinea-Bissau, Cape Verde, São Tomé
territory_changes.description: Independence of African colonies following the Carnation Revolution in Portugal (1974). Colonial wars had lasted 13 years.
Transfer of Macau sovereignty to the People's Republic of China, ending the Portuguese Empire

sources.citation: Bethencourt, F. & Curto, D.R. (2007). Portuguese Oceanic Expansion, 1400-1800. Cambridge University Press.
Newitt, M. (2005). A History of Portuguese Overseas Expansion, 1400-1668. Routledge.
Joint Declaration on the Question of Macau (1987), Government of Portugal and Government of PRC

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The French Colonial Empire was the second largest in the world. It was responsible for extensive exploitation, forced labor, and cultural suppression across Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Caribbean. The Algerian War (1954-1962) involved widespread torture, civilian massacres, and displacement. The boundary_geojson represents metropolitan France only; colonial territories spanned multiple continents.

name_variants.name: Second empire colonial français
name_variants.context: The second phase of French colonialism beginning with the conquest of Algeria
name_variants.source: Coquery-Vidrovitch, C. (2003). Le Congo au temps des grandes compagnies concessionnaires

name_variants.name: France d'outre-mer
name_variants.source: Constitution of the Fourth French Republic (1946)

territory_changes.description: French invasion and colonization of Algeria, lasting 132 years
French defeat at Dien Bien Phu; end of French Indochina
Algerian independence after 8-year war. French forces used torture and resettlement. An estimated 300,000-1,500,000 Algerians died.

territory_changes.region: French West Africa, French Equatorial Africa
territory_changes.description: Year of Africa: 14 former French colonies gained independence

sources.citation: Conklin, A.L. (1997). A Mission to Civilize: The Republican Idea of Empire in France and West Africa. Stanford University Press.
Stora, B. (2001). Algeria 1830-2000: A Short History. Cornell University Press.
Evian Accords (1962), ceasefire agreements between France and the Provisional Government of the Algerian Republic

name_original: État indépendant du Congo / Congo belge
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Congo Free State (1885-1908) under King Leopold II of Belgium was one of the worst cases of colonial atrocity in history. The population was subjected to forced labor for rubber extraction, with mutilation (cutting off hands) as punishment. An estimated 10 million Congolese died. After international outcry, the colony was transferred to the Belgian state in 1908 but exploitation continued. This entity MUST always be presented with full documentation of these atrocities.

name_variants.name: Congo Free State
name_variants.context: English name for Leopold II's personal colony

name_variants.name: Belgian Congo
name_variants.context: English name after transfer to Belgian state

name_variants.name: Kongo ya Belge
name_variants.context: Lingala name used by Congolese population

territory_changes.description: Berlin Conference assigns Congo Basin as personal property of King Leopold II of Belgium
Systematic forced labor regime for rubber extraction. Mass murder, mutilation, and hostage-taking cause estimated 10 million deaths over 23 years.
Transfer of Congo from Leopold II's personal ownership to Belgian state following international scandal
Independence of the Republic of the Congo (later Zaire, then DRC). Patrice Lumumba becomes first elected prime minister.

sources.citation: Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror, and Heroism in Colonial Africa. Houghton Mifflin.
Casement, R. (1904). Casement Report to the British Foreign Office on the Congo Free State
Vansina, J. (2010). Being Colonized: The Kuba Experience in Rural Congo, 1880-1960. University of Wisconsin Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Dutch East Indies was built on centuries of exploitation by the VOC and the Dutch state. The Cultivation System (cultuurstelsel, 1830-1870) was a form of forced labor. The Dutch colonial army committed mass violence in the Aceh War (1873-1914) and other campaigns. Indonesian independence was declared in 1945 but the Dutch fought a brutal colonial war (1945-1949) before recognizing sovereignty.

name_variants.name: Hindia Belanda
name_variants.context: Indonesian name for the colonial territory
name_variants.source: Ricklefs, M.C. (2008). A History of Modern Indonesia

territory_changes.description: Dutch state assumes control of VOC territories across the Indonesian archipelago
Implementation of the Cultivation System (cultuurstelsel): forced labor regime requiring peasants to dedicate portion of land to export crops
Proclamation of Indonesian independence by Sukarno and Hatta. Netherlands initially refuses recognition.
Netherlands recognizes Indonesian sovereignty at the Hague Round Table Conference, after four years of military conflict

sources.citation: Ricklefs, M.C. (2008). A History of Modern Indonesia Since c. 1200. Palgrave Macmillan.
Linggadjati Agreement (1946) and Hague Round Table Conference (1949)
Van den Doel, W. (1996). Het Rijk van Insulinde: Opkomst en ondergang van een Nederlandse kolonie. Prometheus.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Republic of India was born from the Partition of British India (1947), one of the largest mass migrations in history, with approximately 1-2 million deaths and 15 million displaced. Post-independence, India has faced communal violence, the Kashmir dispute (contested with Pakistan), and the controversial 1984 anti-Sikh pogroms. The boundary shown includes Indian-administered Kashmir; Pakistan and China claim portions of this territory.

name_variants.name: Republic of India
name_variants.context: Official English name as per Constitution

territory_changes.description: Partition of British India into India and Pakistan along religious lines. Approximately 1-2 million killed in communal violence, 15 million displaced.
First Kashmir War; India secures most of Kashmir including the valley; Line of Control established
Indian military annexation of Portuguese India
Annexation of the Kingdom of Sikkim as the 22nd state of India, following a referendum

sources.citation: Guha, R. (2007). India After Gandhi: The History of the World's Largest Democracy. Ecco.
Indian Independence Act (1947), UK Parliament
Khan, Y. (2007). The Great Partition: The Making of India and Pakistan. Yale University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Pakistan was born from the Partition of British India (1947) with massive communal violence. In 1971, the Pakistani military operation in East Pakistan (now Bangladesh) constituted genocide, with estimates of 300,000 to 3 million Bengali civilians killed. Pakistan-administered Kashmir (Azad Kashmir) is claimed by India.

name_variants.name: Islamic Republic of Pakistan
name_variants.context: Official English name since adoption of first constitution

territory_changes.region: West Punjab, Sindh, NWFP, Balochistan, East Bengal
territory_changes.description: Partition of British India; creation of Pakistan with two geographically separated wings. Mass communal violence, 1-2 million killed, 15 million displaced.
Pakistani military operation in East Pakistan targeting Bengali civilians, intellectuals, and Hindus. Estimated 300,000-3,000,000 killed. Led to the independence of Bangladesh.
Loss of East Pakistan, which becomes independent Bangladesh after Indian military intervention

sources.citation: Jalal, A. (2014). The Struggle for Pakistan: A Muslim Homeland and Global Politics. Harvard University Press.
Hamoodur Rahman Commission Report (1974), Government of Pakistan (declassified 2000)
Bass, G. (2013). The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide. Knopf.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Bangladesh's independence was preceded by the 1971 genocide in East Pakistan, in which the Pakistani military targeted Bengali civilians, intellectuals, and Hindu minorities. The conflict created 10 million refugees. The number of fatalities is highly disputed, ranging from 300,000 to 3,000,000.

territory_changes.description: Declaration of independence from Pakistan on 26 March 1971, achieved through armed liberation struggle with Indian military support. The conflict included genocide against Bengali civilians.
Operation Searchlight and subsequent Pakistani military campaign targeting Bengali civilians, intellectuals, Hindu minorities. 10 million refugees fled to India.

sources.citation: Sisson, R. & Rose, L. (1990). War and Secession: Pakistan, India, and the Creation of Bangladesh. University of California Press.
Instrument of Surrender (16 December 1971), signed by Lt. Gen. A.A.K. Niazi
Bose, S. (2011). Dead Reckoning: Memories of the 1971 Bangladesh War. Columbia University Press.

name_original: Việt Nam Dân chủ Cộng hòa
ethical_notes: ETHICS: North Vietnam fought two prolonged wars — against French colonialism (1946-1954) and against the United States and South Vietnam (1955-1975). The US dropped more bombs on Vietnam than the total used in WWII. Agent Orange caused widespread environmental devastation and birth defects. Both sides committed atrocities; civilian casualties were enormous.

name_variants.name: Bắc Việt
name_variants.source: Duiker, W. (2000). Ho Chi Minh: A Life

territory_changes.description: Declaration of independence by Ho Chi Minh following Japanese surrender; beginning of anti-colonial struggle against France
Geneva Accords partition Vietnam at the 17th parallel; North Vietnam established as DRV
Fall of Saigon; North Vietnamese forces capture South Vietnam, ending the Vietnam War

sources.citation: Duiker, W. (2000). Ho Chi Minh: A Life. Hyperion.
Geneva Accords (1954), Final Declaration of the Geneva Conference
Turley, W. (2009). The Second Indochina War: A Concise Political and Military History. Rowman & Littlefield.

name_original: Cộng hòa Xã hội chủ nghĩa Việt Nam
ethical_notes: ETHICS: United Vietnam inherited the devastation of decades of war. Post-1975 re-education camps held hundreds of thousands of former South Vietnamese officials. The Boat People crisis (1975-1990s) saw hundreds of thousands flee, many perishing at sea. The country also carries the ongoing legacy of Agent Orange contamination.

territory_changes.description: Formal reunification of North and South Vietnam into the Socialist Republic of Vietnam
Vietnamese invasion of Cambodia, ending the Khmer Rouge genocide regime. Vietnamese forces remained until 1989.

sources.citation: SarDesai, D.R. (2005). Vietnam: Past and Present. Westview Press.
Constitution of the Socialist Republic of Vietnam (1992, amended 2001, 2013)
Kerkvliet, B.J.T. (2005). The Power of Everyday Politics: How Vietnamese Peasants Transformed National Policy. Cornell University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The State of Israel was established in 1948 in a context of the Nakba — the displacement of approximately 700,000 Palestinians from their homes. This entity is marked as 'disputed' because its borders, especially regarding the West Bank, Gaza Strip, East Jerusalem, and the Golan Heights, remain contested under international law. The existing entity 'فلسطين / ישראל' in batch_00 covers the broader territorial dispute; this entity represents the state as declared. Palestinian, Israeli, and international perspectives must all be documented.

territory_changes.description: Declaration of Independence and 1948 Arab-Israeli War. Approximately 700,000 Palestinians displaced in the Nakba (catastrophe). Israel controls more territory than the UN Partition Plan allocated.

territory_changes.region: West Bank, Gaza, Sinai, Golan Heights
territory_changes.description: Six-Day War: Israel occupies West Bank (from Jordan), Gaza Strip and Sinai (from Egypt), Golan Heights (from Syria). Beginning of ongoing military occupation.
Return of Sinai to Egypt under the Camp David Accords and Egypt-Israel Peace Treaty

sources.citation: Morris, B. (2004). The Birth of the Palestinian Refugee Problem Revisited. Cambridge University Press.
Israeli Declaration of Independence (14 May 1948)
Khalidi, R. (2020). The Hundred Years' War on Palestine. Metropolitan Books.

ethical_notes: Egypt's modern history includes significant political repression under various regimes. The Suez Crisis (1956) was a pivotal anti-colonial moment. The 2011 revolution and subsequent military coup (2013) remain contested political events.

territory_changes.description: Abolition of the monarchy and declaration of the Republic following the 1952 Free Officers Revolution led by Naguib and Nasser
Suez Crisis: Egypt nationalizes the Suez Canal, repelling British-French-Israeli invasion with US and Soviet pressure
Israel occupies Sinai during the Six-Day War; Egypt loses sovereignty over the peninsula

sources.citation: Vatikiotis, P.J. (1991). The History of Modern Egypt: From Muhammad Ali to Mubarak. Johns Hopkins University Press.
Nasser, G.A. (1954). The Philosophy of the Revolution
Hourani, A. (1991). A History of the Arab Peoples. Harvard University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Republic of Turkey was established on territory from which the Armenian, Assyrian, and Greek populations had been largely eliminated through the Armenian Genocide (1915-1923), the Assyrian Genocide, and the population exchange with Greece (1923). The Turkish state does not officially recognize the Armenian Genocide. Kurdish populations have faced cultural suppression and military operations. These facts must be documented alongside the Ottoman entity.

name_variants.name: Türkiye
name_variants.context: Turkish short name; in 2022 Turkey requested the UN use Türkiye internationally

territory_changes.description: Treaty of Lausanne establishes the Republic of Turkey from the remnants of the Ottoman Empire, with internationally recognized borders
Compulsory population exchange between Greece and Turkey under the Treaty of Lausanne. Approximately 1.5 million Greek Orthodox and 500,000 Muslims forcibly relocated.
Turkish military invasion and occupation of Northern Cyprus following the Greek-backed coup against the Cypriot government

sources.citation: Zürcher, E.J. (2004). Turkey: A Modern History. I.B. Tauris.
Treaty of Lausanne (1923), Conference of Lausanne
Akçam, T. (2006). A Shameful Act: The Armenian Genocide and the Question of Turkish Responsibility. Metropolitan Books.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The PRC's history includes the Great Leap Forward (1958-1962) which caused an estimated 15-55 million famine deaths, the Cultural Revolution (1966-1976) with widespread political persecution, the Tiananmen Square massacre (1989), and ongoing repression in Tibet, Xinjiang (Uyghur forced labor and mass detention), and Hong Kong. The Xinjiang situation has been described as genocide by multiple governments and human rights organizations.

territory_changes.description: Chinese Communist Party under Mao Zedong defeats Kuomintang, establishes the People's Republic of China
PLA invasion and annexation of Tibet; the Seventeen Point Agreement was signed under duress
Great Leap Forward: forced collectivization and industrialization policies cause famine killing an estimated 15-55 million people
Transfer of Hong Kong sovereignty from the United Kingdom to PRC under the Sino-British Joint Declaration

sources.citation: Dikötter, F. (2010). Mao's Great Famine: The History of China's Most Devastating Catastrophe. Walker & Company.
Sino-British Joint Declaration (1984) on the Question of Hong Kong
Spence, J. (1999). The Search for Modern China. W.W. Norton.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Republic of China (ROC) entity is complex: founded in 1912 on mainland China, the ROC government retreated to Taiwan in 1949. Its status is one of the most sensitive geopolitical disputes globally. The PRC claims Taiwan as its territory; Taiwan functions as an independent state but lacks widespread diplomatic recognition. The White Terror period (1947-1987) involved political repression. The existing 臺灣 / Taiwan entity in batch_00 covers the broader territorial status; this entity documents the ROC as a political entity.

name_variants.name: Taiwan
name_variants.context: Commonly used English name for the ROC after retreat to Taiwan

name_variants.name: Formosa
name_variants.context: Portuguese historical name for the island

territory_changes.description: Xinhai Revolution overthrows the Qing Dynasty; Republic of China established under Sun Yat-sen
228 Incident: KMT government suppression of anti-government uprising in Taiwan; estimated 18,000-28,000 Taiwanese killed, beginning the White Terror period
KMT government retreats to Taiwan after losing Chinese Civil War to CCP. ROC authority reduced to Taiwan, Penghu, Kinmen, and Matsu.

sources.citation: Manthorpe, J. (2005). Forbidden Nation: A History of Taiwan. Palgrave Macmillan.
ROC Constitution (1947), adopted by the National Assembly
Rubinstein, M. (2007). Taiwan: A New History. M.E. Sharpe.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Iraq's modern history includes the Ba'athist regime's Anfal campaign (1986-1989), which constituted genocide against the Kurdish population with chemical weapons attacks including Halabja. The 2003 US-led invasion and subsequent occupation caused massive civilian casualties and destabilization. The rise of ISIS (2014) brought additional atrocities including genocide against Yazidis.

territory_changes.description: 14 July Revolution overthrows the Hashemite monarchy; Republic of Iraq established

Anfal campaign: Ba'athist regime under Saddam Hussein conducts genocide against Kurds using chemical weapons. Halabja attack kills 5,000 in a single day. Estimated 50,000-182,000 Kurdish civilians killed.
US-led coalition invasion and occupation of Iraq; overthrow of Ba'athist government. Estimated 150,000-600,000+ Iraqi civilian deaths in subsequent years.
ISIS genocide against Yazidi population in Sinjar. Thousands killed, thousands of women and girls enslaved.

sources.citation: Tripp, C. (2007). A History of Iraq. Cambridge University Press.
Anfal Trial Proceedings, Iraqi High Tribunal (2006-2007)
Human Rights Watch (1993). Genocide in Iraq: The Anfal Campaign Against the Kurds.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Libya experienced Italian colonial atrocities (1911-1943) including concentration camps and mass displacement of Bedouin populations. Under Gaddafi (1969-2011), political repression was severe. The 2011 NATO intervention and Gaddafi's overthrow led to a protracted civil war and state fragmentation. Libya is marked as 'disputed' due to competing governments since 2014.

name_variants.name: الجماهيرية العربية الليبية الشعبية الاشتراكية العظمى
name_variants.context: Great Socialist People's Libyan Arab Jamahiriya — Gaddafi-era official name
name_variants.source: Libyan Declaration of the Establishment of the Authority of the People (1977)

territory_changes.description: Independence of the United Kingdom of Libya under King Idris, ending Italian colonial and subsequent British/French administration
Military coup by Muammar Gaddafi overthrows King Idris; establishment of the Libyan Arab Republic
Libyan Civil War and NATO intervention; overthrow and killing of Gaddafi. Country subsequently fragments into competing governments.

sources.citation: Vandewalle, D. (2012). A History of Modern Libya. Cambridge University Press.
UN Security Council Resolution 1973 (2011), authorizing military intervention in Libya
St John, R.B. (2012). Libya: From Colony to Revolution. Oneworld Publications.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The DRC's post-independence history is marked by external interference (CIA-backed assassination of Lumumba, 1961), Mobutu's 32-year kleptocratic dictatorship, and the First and Second Congo Wars (1996-2003) — the deadliest conflict since WWII with an estimated 5.4 million deaths. Ongoing conflict in eastern DRC involves mass violence against civilians, including widespread sexual violence as a weapon of war.

name_variants.name: Congo-Kinshasa
name_variants.context: Used to distinguish from Republic of Congo (Brazzaville)

name_variants.name: Jamhuri ya Kidemokrasia ya Kongo
name_variants.context: Swahili name, one of the national languages

territory_changes.description: Independence from Belgium. Patrice Lumumba becomes first democratically elected prime minister.
Katanga secession crisis (1960-1963); assassination of Lumumba with Belgian and CIA involvement
Second Congo War (Africa's World War): involving 9 African nations. Estimated 5.4 million excess deaths from violence, disease, and starvation by 2008.

sources.citation: Stearns, J.K. (2011). Dancing in the Glory of Monsters: The Collapse of the Congo and the Great War of Africa. PublicAffairs.
Lumumba, P. (1960). Independence Day Speech, 30 June 1960
Prunier, G. (2009). Africa's World War: Congo, the Rwandan Genocide, and the Making of a Continental Catastrophe. Oxford University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Islamic Republic was established through the 1979 revolution. The post-revolution period included mass executions of political prisoners (1988 prison massacres), the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988) with massive casualties, persecution of religious minorities (Baha'is in particular), and ongoing repression of women's rights and political dissent, including the Mahsa Amini protests (2022).

name_variants.name: Iran
name_variants.context: English short name; Iran requested international use of 'Iran' instead of 'Persia' in 1935

territory_changes.description: Islamic Revolution overthrows the Pahlavi monarchy; Ayatollah Khomeini establishes the Islamic Republic
Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988): Iraqi invasion of Iran's Khuzestan province; eight-year war causing an estimated 500,000-1,000,000 combined deaths, including widespread use of chemical weapons by Iraq

sources.citation: Abrahamian, E. (2008). A History of Modern Iran. Cambridge University Press.
Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran (1979, amended 1989)
Axworthy, M. (2013). Revolutionary Iran: A History of the Islamic Republic. Oxford University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: East Germany was a Soviet satellite state with extensive political repression through the Stasi (Ministerium für Staatssicherheit). Citizens attempting to cross the Berlin Wall and inner German border were shot under the Schießbefehl (shoot-to-kill order). An estimated 140 people died at the Berlin Wall alone.

territory_changes.description: Establishment of the DDR from the Soviet Occupation Zone of Germany
Construction of the Berlin Wall, physically dividing East and West Berlin to prevent mass emigration
German reunification: DDR dissolves and its territory is absorbed into the Federal Republic of Germany

sources.citation: Fulbrook, M. (2005). The People's State: East German Society from Hitler to Honecker. Yale University Press.
Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany (Two Plus Four Agreement, 1990)
Koehler, J. (1999). Stasi: The Untold Story of the East German Secret Police. Westview Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: North Korea is one of the most repressive states in the world. The political prison camp system (kwanliso) holds an estimated 80,000-120,000 inmates under conditions constituting crimes against humanity. A UN Commission of Inquiry (2014) found systematic human rights violations including extermination, murder, enslavement, torture, and rape. The Korean War (1950-1953) caused approximately 3 million civilian deaths.

territory_changes.description: Establishment of the DPRK in the Soviet-occupied zone of Korea, north of the 38th parallel
North Korean invasion of South Korea triggers the Korean War (1950-1953). Approximately 3 million civilians killed; massive destruction of infrastructure on both sides.

sources.citation: UN Commission of Inquiry on Human Rights in the DPRK (2014). Report A/HRC/25/CRP.1
Cumings, B. (2010). The Korean War: A History. Modern Library.
Lankov, A. (2013). The Real North Korea: Life and Politics in the Failed Stalinist Utopia. Oxford University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Communist Albania under Enver Hoxha (1944-1985) was one of the most isolated and repressive states in Europe. Political repression included labor camps, executions, and the banning of all religious practice (the world's first officially atheist state). Hoxha's paranoia led to the construction of approximately 173,000 bunkers. The regime collapsed in 1991-1992.

territory_changes.description: Communist partisans under Enver Hoxha liberate Albania from Nazi occupation without Allied assistance
Albania breaks with the Soviet Union, aligning with China; becomes the most isolated state in Europe
End of communist rule; transition to multiparty democracy

sources.citation: Vickers, M. (1999). The Albanians: A Modern History. I.B. Tauris.
Albanian Constitution (1976), People's Socialist Republic of Albania
Fischer, B.J. (1999). Albania at War, 1939-1945. Purdue University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Communist Poland was a Soviet satellite state. Poland's post-WWII borders reflect massive population transfers: ethnic Germans expelled from western territories (Silesia, Pomerania, East Prussia) and Poles expelled from eastern territories (Kresy) annexed by the USSR. The Solidarity movement (1980) was the first independent trade union in the Soviet bloc.

territory_changes.description: Poland receives former German territories east of the Oder-Neisse line; approximately 12 million Germans expelled from these territories
Poland cedes eastern territories (Lwów, Wilno regions) to the Soviet Union; approximately 2 million Poles forcibly resettled
Round Table negotiations between Solidarity and communist government lead to partially free elections and peaceful end of communist rule

sources.citation: Prazmowska, A. (2010). Poland: A Modern History. I.B. Tauris.
Potsdam Agreement (1945), Protocol of the Proceedings, Section IX: Poland
Ash, T.G. (2002). The Polish Revolution: Solidarity. Yale University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Rhodesia was an internationally unrecognized state established through a Unilateral Declaration of Independence (UDI) by a white minority government representing less than 5% of the population. The regime enforced racial segregation and excluded the Black majority from political participation. The subsequent Bush War (1964-1979) caused thousands of deaths. This entity must be presented alongside the perspectives of the liberation movements (ZANU, ZAPU) and the Black majority.

name_variants.name: Rhodesia
name_variants.context: Self-proclaimed name after UDI; internationally unrecognized

name_variants.name: Southern Rhodesia
name_variants.context: Colonial name as self-governing British colony

territory_changes.description: Unilateral Declaration of Independence from the United Kingdom by the white minority government of Ian Smith; internationally condemned and unrecognized
UN Security Council imposes comprehensive mandatory sanctions; liberation war (Bush War) intensifies
Lancaster House Agreement leads to free elections; Robert Mugabe elected; Zimbabwe independent under majority rule

sources.citation: Meredith, M. (2007). Mugabe: Power, Plunder, and the Struggle for Zimbabwe. PublicAffairs.
Lancaster House Agreement (1979), United Kingdom Government
Ranger, T. (1985). Peasant Consciousness and Guerrilla War in Zimbabwe. James Currey.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Apartheid South Africa enforced a system of institutionalized racial segregation and white supremacy from 1948 to 1994, classified as a crime against humanity by the UN. The regime denied political rights to the Black majority, forcibly relocated millions into Bantustans (homelands), and committed violence including the Sharpeville massacre (1960) and Soweto uprising (1976). The system was dismantled through the struggle of the ANC and other liberation movements, negotiations, and the leadership of Nelson Mandela.

name_variants.name: Apartheid South Africa
name_variants.context: Common English historiographic designation for the apartheid-era state

name_variants.name: Republic of South Africa
name_variants.context: Official English name after becoming a republic
name_variants.source: Republic of South Africa Constitution Act (1961)

name_variants.name: Unie van Suid-Afrika
name_variants.context: Afrikaans name during the Union period

territory_changes.description: National Party wins election; begins implementing apartheid — a system of institutionalized racial segregation affecting the entire non-white population
Sharpeville massacre: police kill 69 unarmed Black protesters against pass laws; marks escalation of anti-apartheid resistance and international condemnation
Soweto uprising: police fire on schoolchildren protesting mandatory Afrikaans education; at least 176 killed, sparking nationwide resistance
First free and fair elections; Nelson Mandela elected president. End of apartheid and beginning of multiracial democracy.

sources.citation: Clark, N.L. & Worger, W.H. (2011). South Africa: The Rise and Fall of Apartheid. Routledge.
Truth and Reconciliation Commission of South Africa Report (1998)
Mandela, N. (1994). Long Walk to Freedom. Little, Brown and Company.

name_original: កម្ពុជាប្រជាធិបតេយ្យ
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Democratic Kampuchea (the Khmer Rouge regime) committed genocide from 1975 to 1979, killing an estimated 1.5 to 2 million people out of a population of approximately 8 million — roughly 25% of the population. Methods included mass execution, forced labor, starvation, and torture. S-21 (Tuol Sleng) prison and the Killing Fields are emblematic of the regime's brutality. Targeted groups included ethnic Vietnamese, Chinese, Cham Muslims, Buddhist monks, intellectuals, and urban residents. The Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia (ECCC) convicted senior leaders of genocide.

name_variants.name: Democratic Kampuchea
name_variants.context: Official English name used by the Khmer Rouge regime

name_variants.name: Khmer Rouge Cambodia
name_variants.context: Common English designation referencing the ruling movement
name_variants.source: Kiernan, B. (2008). The Pol Pot Regime

territory_changes.description: Khmer Rouge forces capture Phnom Penh on 17 April 1975; immediate forced evacuation of the capital's 2 million residents to the countryside

Khmer Rouge regime under Pol Pot implements radical agrarian communism. Systematic extermination of perceived enemies: intellectuals, ethnic minorities (Vietnamese, Chinese, Cham), religious figures. 1.5-2 million killed in 4 years out of a population of 8 million.
Vietnamese military invasion overthrows the Khmer Rouge regime; People's Republic of Kampuchea established under Vietnamese sponsorship

sources.citation: Kiernan, B. (2008). The Pol Pot Regime: Race, Power, and Genocide in Cambodia under the Khmer Rouge, 1975-79. Yale University Press.
ECCC (Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia), Case 002/02 (2018) — Judgment finding genocide
Chandler, D. (1999). Brother Number One: A Political Biography of Pol Pot. Westview Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Cyprus has been divided since the 1974 Turkish military invasion, following a Greek-backed coup. The island is split between the internationally recognized Republic of Cyprus (Greek Cypriot) and the Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus (recognized only by Turkey). Ethnic cleansing occurred on both sides during the 1974 crisis. Both Greek and Turkish Cypriot perspectives must be represented. The existing TRNC entity in batch_00 covers the northern state.

territory_changes.description: Independence from British colonial rule under the Zurich and London Agreements; power-sharing arrangement between Greek and Turkish Cypriots

Turkish military invasion following Greek-backed coup. Approximately 200,000 Greek Cypriots displaced from the north; 50,000 Turkish Cypriots displaced from the south. Island divided along the Green Line.

sources.citation: Ker-Lindsay, J. (2011). The Cyprus Problem: What Everyone Needs to Know. Oxford University Press.
Zurich and London Agreements (1959), establishing the Republic of Cyprus
Papadakis, Y. (2005). Echoes from the Dead Zone: Across the Cyprus Divide. I.B. Tauris.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Communist Hungary was a Soviet satellite state. The 1956 Hungarian Revolution was a pivotal anti-Soviet uprising crushed by Soviet military intervention, killing approximately 2,500 Hungarians and causing 200,000 to flee. Hungary's post-Trianon (1920) borders removed two-thirds of its territory and left large Hungarian minorities in neighboring countries, a source of ongoing tension.

territory_changes.description: Communist takeover: establishment of the Hungarian People's Republic under Soviet influence
Hungarian Revolution of 1956: popular uprising against Soviet control. Crushed by Soviet military intervention; approximately 2,500 Hungarians killed, 200,000 refugees flee the country.
Peaceful transition: Hungary opens border with Austria (triggering pan-European changes), Round Table talks lead to the end of communist rule

sources.citation: Gati, C. (2006). Failed Illusions: Moscow, Washington, Budapest, and the 1956 Hungarian Revolt. Stanford University Press.
Hungarian Constitution (1949), Act XX of 1949
Kontler, L. (2002). A History of Hungary. Palgrave Macmillan.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Communist Romania under Ceaușescu (1965-1989) imposed extreme repression including the systematization program (forced urbanization and demolition of villages), the ban on abortion and contraception leading to massive numbers of abandoned children in state orphanages, and the persecution of the Hungarian minority. The 1989 revolution was the only violent overthrow of a communist regime in Eastern Europe, with over 1,100 killed.

territory_changes.description: King Michael forced to abdicate; Romanian People's Republic proclaimed under Soviet-backed communist party

Romania forced to cede Northern Transylvania to Hungary (Vienna Award), Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina to the USSR, and Southern Dobruja to Bulgaria. Northern Transylvania returned in 1945; Bessarabia remained Soviet.
Romanian Revolution: the only violent overthrow of a communist government in Eastern Europe. Ceaușescu executed. Over 1,100 killed in the revolution.

sources.citation: Deletant, D. (1999). Communist Terror in Romania: Gheorghiu-Dej and the Police State, 1948-1965. C. Hurst & Co.
Romanian Constitution (1965), Socialist Republic of Romania
Siani-Davies, P. (2005). The Romanian Revolution of December 1989. Cornell University Press.

ethical_notes: ETHICS: South Sudan gained independence in 2011 after decades of civil war in Sudan that killed an estimated 2 million people. However, the new state descended into civil war in 2013 along ethnic lines (primarily Dinka vs Nuer), causing approximately 400,000 deaths and 4 million displaced by 2020. Ethnic massacres, sexual violence as a weapon of war, and the use of child soldiers have been extensively documented.

territory_changes.description: Independence referendum (98.83% vote for separation) leads to independence from Sudan on 9 July 2011; world's newest country
South Sudanese Civil War erupts along ethnic lines. Mass atrocities including targeted ethnic killings, rape, and displacement. Estimated 400,000 deaths by 2020.

sources.citation: Johnson, D.H. (2016). South Sudan: A New History for a New Nation. Ohio University Press.
Comprehensive Peace Agreement (2005), Government of Sudan and Sudan People's Liberation Movement/Army
African Union Commission of Inquiry on South Sudan (2015), Final Report

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Republic of Serbian Krajina was a self-proclaimed Serb entity within Croatia during the Yugoslav Wars. Its creation involved the ethnic cleansing of Croat and other non-Serb populations from the occupied territories. Its end came through Operation Storm (1995), during which Croatian forces recaptured the territory, causing the displacement of approximately 200,000 ethnic Serbs — the largest single act of ethnic cleansing in the Yugoslav Wars. Both sides committed war crimes; all perspectives must be documented.

territory_changes.description: Serbian-majority areas in Croatia declare independence; backed by the Yugoslav People's Army. Ethnic cleansing of non-Serb populations in occupied areas.
Operation Storm: Croatian forces recapture Krajina in largest European land military operation since WWII. Approximately 200,000 Serbs flee or are expelled. Classified as ethnic cleansing by ICTY.

sources.citation: Silber, L. & Little, A. (1997). Yugoslavia: Death of a Nation. Penguin.
ICTY, Prosecutor v. Gotovina et al., Case IT-06-90 (2011)
Hoare, M.A. (2010). The Bosnian Muslims in the Second World War. Oxford University Press.
